papers.title: Multi-label emotion analysis in conversation via multimodal knowledge distillation
papers.authors: Sidharth Anand∗,Naresh Kumar Devulapally∗,Sreyasee Das Bhattacharjee,Junsong Yuan
papers.summary_motivation:
Addresses the limitations of single-dominant emotion assumptions by tackling the challenge of multi-label emotion co-occurrence and generalization across diverse socio-demographic groups, particularly varying age populations.
[翻译] 针对现有多模态方法主要关注单一主导情感的局限性，该研究致力于解决情感标签共现的识别难题，并提升模型在不同社会人口统计学群体（特别是不同年龄段人群）中的泛化能力。
papers.summary_innovation: 将多模态知识蒸馏与标签一致性校准损失（LCC）相结合，减轻了模型对简单标签的过拟合（保证置信度相近）；构建了一个利用蒸馏方法的整体框架，其目的是为了融合各模态能力
papers.summary_method:
Employing a Multimodal Transformer Network where mode-specific peer branches (visual, audio, textual) collaboratively distill learned probabilistic uncertainty into a fusion branch via cross-network attention and noise contrastive estimation.……
[翻译] 将三个特定模态的对等分支通过跨网络注意力和噪声对比估计，协同地将其学习到的概率蒸馏到融合分支中，构建了一个拥有四个分支的整体预测模型。[值得关注]视频使用Tubelet embedding技术，将视频切分为时空小块（Spatial-Temporal Tubes），保留时空信息
papers.summary_conclusion:
Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on MOSEI, EmoReact, and ElderReact datasets, achieving an approximate 17% improvement in weighted F1-score during cross-dataset evaluations, thereby validating robustness in age-diverse scenarios.
[翻译] 在MOSEI、EmoReact和ElderReact数据集上最先进的性能，跨数据集评估有约17%的加权F1提升，在跨年龄场景下具有鲁棒性。
papers.summary_limitation:
The approach necessitates the reduction of complex emotion categories to basic subsets for cross-dataset consistency and entails significant computational overhead due to the spatiotemporal tubelet embedding mechanism.
[翻译] 为了保持跨数据集一致性，要将复杂情感类归约为基础子集，由于采用时空Tubelet嵌入机制，导致了显著的计算开销
papers.conference: Proceedings of the 31st ACM International Conference on Multimedia
papers.title_translation: [AI generated] **中文标题：** 基于多模态知识蒸馏的对话多标签情感分析话多标签情感分析
papers.analogy_summary: 三个专家分别处理一个模态，训练的同时将能力蒸馏给融合分支，最终形成一个整体模型，教师（分支专家）与学生（融合专家）一同处理多模态内容，得到情感分类
papers.abstract: Evaluating speaker emotion in conversations is crucial for various applications requiring human-computer interaction. However, co-occurrences of multiple emotional states (e.g. 'anger' and 'frustration' may occur together or one may influence the occurrence of the other) and their dynamic evolution may vary dramatically due to the speaker's internal (e.g., influence of their personalized socio-cultural-educational and demographic backgrounds) and external contexts. Thus far, the previous focus has been on evaluating only the dominant emotion observed in a speaker at a given time, which is susceptible to producing misleading classification decisions for difficult multi-labels during testing. In this work, we present Self-supervised Multi- Label Peer Collaborative Distillation (SeMuL-PCD) Learning via an efficient Multimodal Transformer Network, in which complementary feedback from multiple mode-specific peer networks (e.g.transcript, audio, visual) are distilled into a single mode-ensembled fusion network for estimating multiple emotions simultaneously. The proposed Multimodal Distillation Loss calibrates the fusion network by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence with the peer networks. Additionally, each peer network is conditioned using a self-supervised contrastive objective to improve the generalization across diverse socio-demographic speaker backgrounds. By enabling peer collaborative learning that allows each network to independently learn their mode-specific discriminative patterns,SeMUL-PCD is effective across different conversation environments. In particular, the model not only outperforms the current state-of-the-art models on several large-scale public datasets (e.g., MOSEI, EmoReact and ElderReact), but with around 17% improved weighted F1-score in the cross-dataset experimental settings. The model also demonstrates an impressive generalization ability across age and demography-diverse populations.
papers.notes:
【面向结果模型训练】[引用句]"Transscending the traditional paradigm of identifying single dominant emotions, Anand et al. [2023] proposed SeMuL-PCD to enhance the granularity of affective perception in diverse social contexts; by leveraging a collaborative distillation mechanism that calibrates mode-specific feedback, their model robustly disentangles multi-label emotional co-occurrences across varying demographic backgrounds (e.g., children and the elderly), thereby providing a more nuanced foundation for socially adaptive agents."
[翻译] “为了超越识别单一主导情感的传统范式，Anand等人[2023]提出了SeMuL-PCD，旨在增强不同社会语境下情感感知的粒度；通过利用一种校准模态特定反馈的协作蒸馏机制，该模型能够在不同的人口统计背景（如儿童和老人）下鲁棒地解耦多标签情感的共现关系，从而为具备社会适应能力的智能体提供了更精细的情感理解基础。”

papers.title: MIND: A Multi-agent Framework for Zero-shot Harmful Meme Detection
papers.authors: Ziyan Liu, Chunxiao Fan, Haoran Lou, Yuexin Wu, Kaiwei Deng
papers.summary_motivation: The primary motivation of this paper is to address the challenge of detecting harmful memes in a zero-shot setting, i.e., without relying on any annotated training data. Traditional data-driven methods struggle because memes evolve rapidly and often lack up-to-date labeled examples. To overcome this, the authors propose MIND, a multi-agent framework that retrieves similar unlabeled memes, derives bidirectional insights, and uses a multi-agent debate mechanism to make robust harmfulness judgments. The goal is to create an adaptable, scalable solution for harmful meme detection that performs well even on emerging or unseen content.
papers.summary_innovation:
Zero-shot harmful meme detection without annotated data: Unlike prior data-driven or few-shot approaches, MIND eliminates the need for labeled training data, addressing the critical challenge of rapidly evolving meme content.

Multi-agent collaborative framework: It introduces a novel architecture with three stages—Similar Sample Retrieval, Bidirectional Insight Derivation, and Multi-agent Debate—that mimics human collaborative analysis to robustly assess harmfulness.

Bidirectional insight derivation: By processing similar memes in both forward and backward orders, the framework ensures each retrieved meme contributes equally, mitigating order bias and capturing more comprehensive patterns.

Debate-based reasoning with a judge agent: The use of two debater agents with complementary perspectives, plus a judge agent for arbitration, improves decision reliability and reduces individual biases.

Strong empirical results and generalization: MIND consistently outperforms existing zero-shot baselines across three datasets and generalizes well across different model architectures and scales (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-7B to Gemini-1.5-Flash), demonstrating its scalability and adaptability.
papers.summary_method:
Similar Sample Retrieval (SSR): For a target meme, it retrieves the top‑
K
K most similar memes from an unlabeled reference set using multimodal embeddings (combining visual and textual features via CLIP).

Relevant Insight Derivation (RID): A deriving agent processes the retrieved similar memes in both forward and backward orders. In each pass, the agent iteratively analyzes a meme and updates a set of general, high‑level insights (rules) about what makes a meme harmful.

Insight-Augmented Inference (IAI): Two debater agents separately use the forward‑derived and backward‑derived insights to produce reasoning‑based judgments. If the judgments agree, that answer is final; if they disagree, a judge agent arbitrates by examining both reasoning chains and decides the final verdict.
papers.summary_conclusion:
Effective zero‑shot detection without annotated data: The proposed MIND framework successfully detects harmful memes using only unlabeled reference memes, eliminating the need for costly and time‑consuming data annotation.

Superior performance: MIND significantly outperforms existing zero‑shot baselines (including GPT‑4o and LLaVA‑1.6‑34B) across three public meme datasets (HarM, FHM, MAMI), achieving higher accuracy and macro‑F1 scores.

Strong generalization across models: The framework consistently improves performance when applied to different LMMs (LLaVA‑1.5‑7B/13B/34B, Gemini‑1.5‑Flash), demonstrating its architecture‑ and scale‑agnostic effectiveness.

Validation of core strategies: Ablation studies confirm that all three components—Similar Sample Retrieval (SSR), Relevant Insight Derivation (RID), and Insight‑Augmented Inference (IAI)—are essential and complementary for robust detection.

Practical and scalable solution: By operating without training or labeled data, MIND offers a practical, adaptable approach for real‑world social media moderation, especially for rapidly evolving harmful meme content.
papers.summary_limitation:
Limitations
Retrieval quality dependence: The framework’s performance partly relies on the quality of retrieved similar memes. Current embedding‑based similarity may not capture complex or highly contextual meme semantics.

Uniform treatment of retrieved memes: All similar memes are treated equally; no weighting mechanism accounts for varying degrees of relevance.

Lack of insight quality evaluation: There is no quantitative way to assess whether derived insights are reliable, even when final predictions are correct.

High inference cost: MIND requires approximately 8× more computational overhead than simple zero‑shot baselines due to multiple LMM calls, limiting real‑time deployment.

Cultural and nuanced understanding: Performance still depends on the base LMM’s ability to grasp subtle social/cultural cues; may struggle with deeply contextual or evolving harmful content.

Future Work
More sophisticated retrieval strategies: Improve beyond embedding‑based similarity to better capture complex semantic relationships.

Nuanced weighting of retrieved memes: Assign different importance levels to similar memes based on relevance.

Quantitative evaluation of insight quality: Develop methods to systematically assess the reliability of derived reasoning chains.

Efficiency optimization: Explore configurations (e.g., unidirectional RID) to reduce inference time while maintaining performance.

Broader robustness testing: Apply the framework to more diverse meme contexts and evolving harmful patterns.
papers.analogy_summary: 该文章就像组建了一支“专家陪审团”——无需任何标注好的证据（数据），通过检索相似案例、正反双向推敲、相互辩论裁决，从而精准识别出有害的网络梗图。
papers.abstract: The rapid expansion of memes on social media has highlighted the urgent need for effective approaches to detect harmful content. However, traditional data-driven approaches struggle to detect new memes due to their evolving nature and the lack of up-to-date annotated data. To address this issue, we propose MIND, a multi-agent framework for zero-shot harmful meme detection that does not rely on annotated data. MIND implements three key strategies: 1) We retrieve similar memes from an unannotated reference set to provide contextual information. 2) We propose a bidirectional insight derivation mechanism to extract a comprehensive understanding of similar memes. 3) We then employ a multi-agent debate mechanism to ensure robust decisionmaking through reasoned arbitration. Extensive experiments on three meme datasets demonstrate that our proposed framework not only outperforms existing zero-shot approaches but also shows strong generalization across different model architectures and parameter scales, providing a scalable solution for harmful meme detection. The code is available at https: //github.com/destroy-lonely/MIND.

papers.title: Improving Multimodal Hateful Meme Detection Exploiting LMM-Generated Knowledge
papers.summary_motivation: Memes have become a powerful tool for communication, but they are increasingly exploited to promote hate speech. Detecting hateful memes is challenging because their meaning arises from the complex interaction between an image and its embedded text. Traditional methods often struggle to capture these nuances. This paper aims to improve hateful meme detection by leveraging the knowledge encoded in Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), using them in a computationally efficient, inference-only manner to build stronger meme representations and improve the training process.
papers.summary_innovation:
LMM-Generated Representations: It proposes a novel method to build powerful meme representations by extracting not just generic semantic descriptions from an LMM, but also the emotions a meme elicits. This is the first work to exploit such multimodal, LMM-generated knowledge in a fully multimodal fashion for this task.

LMM-Based Hard Mining: It introduces a new auxiliary loss function that uses an LMM to identify "hard" training samples (those the LMM misclassifies). The training process is then regularized to make these hard samples more similar to their nearest correct samples, significantly boosting classification performance.
papers.summary_method:
Build LMM-generated Meme Representations: A frozen LMM (MiniGPT-4) is prompted to generate 10 semantic descriptions and 10 elicited emotions for each meme (image + text). A frozen CLIP model then extracts four embeddings: image, embedded text, semantic descriptions, and elicited emotions. These are concatenated to form the final meme representation.

LMM-based Mining of Hard Samples: The same LMM is prompted to classify each training meme. Its incorrect predictions are designated as "hard samples".

Train a Simple Classification Head: A lightweight classification head (three linear layers) is trained on the meme representations. The loss function combines a standard cross-entropy loss with a novel auxiliary loss that pulls hard samples closer to their correct neighbors in the embedding space while pushing them away from samples of the opposite class.
papers.summary_conclusion:
State-of-the-Art Performance: The proposed method achieves new state-of-the-art results on two challenging hateful meme datasets, Harm-C and PrideMM.

Computational Efficiency: Unlike methods that train or fine-tune LMMs, this approach only uses LMMs for inference and trains a simple classifier, making it significantly more computationally efficient.

Effectiveness of Components: Ablation studies confirm that both the LMM-generated semantic/emotion embeddings and the LMM-mined hard sample loss contribute to the performance gains.
papers.summary_limitation:
Subjectivity of Hate Speech: Defining and detecting hate speech involves a degree of subjectivity, making it difficult to distinguish between offensive and non-offensive content.

Computational Cost: While efficient compared to training LMMs, the method still incurs computational overhead due to using an LMM for inference. Future work could involve experimenting with smaller LMMs for easier deployment on resource-constrained devices.

Multilingual Extension: The paper's application is limited to English memes. Future work could extend the method to multilingual memes, contingent on the availability of effective multilingual vision-language models.
papers.analogy_summary: 该文章就像请一位“多模态专家”（LMM）为每个模因撰写详细的观后感与情绪点评，并用它挑出最难的考题，从而训练出一个既高效又精准的“模因安检员”。
papers.abstract: Memes have become a dominant form of communication in social media in recent years. Memes are typically humorous and harmless, however there are also memes that promote hate speech, being in this way harmful to individuals and groups based on their identity. Therefore, detecting hateful content in memes has emerged as a task of critical importance. The need for understanding the complex interactions of images and their embedded text renders the hateful meme detection a challenging multimodal task. In this paper we propose to address the aforementioned task leveraging knowledge encoded in powerful Large Multimodal Models (LMM). Specifically, we propose to exploit LMMs in a two-fold manner. First, by extracting knowledge oriented to the hateful meme detection task in order to build strong meme representations. Specifically, generic semantic descriptions and emotions that the images along with their embedded texts elicit are extracted, which are then used to train a simple classification head for hateful meme detection. Second, by developing a novel hard mining approach introducing directly LMM-encoded knowledge to the training process, providing further improvements. We perform extensive experiments on two datasets that validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving state-of-theart performance.1 Disclaimer: This paper contains hateful content that may be disturbing to some readers.
papers.notes: Comment: Accepted for publication, Multimodal Learning and Applications Workshop (MULA 2025) @ IEEE/CVF CVPR 2025, Nashville, TN, USA, June 2025. This is the authors' "accepted version"

papers.title: Improving Hateful Meme Detection through Retrieval-Guided Contrastive Learning
papers.authors: Jingbiao Mei, Jinghong Chen, Weizhe Lin, Bill Byrne, Marcus Tomalin
papers.summary_motivation: The main motivation is to solve the challenge of "confounder memes" — subtle differences in images or text that can change a meme's meaning from benign to hateful. Existing CLIP-based models often struggle with these nuances. This paper therefore aims to improve the detection of such nuanced hateful memes.
papers.summary_innovation:
Pseudo-gold positive examples: Same-label samples that are semantically close, pulling similar benign or hateful memes together.

Hard negative examples: Opposite-label samples that are very similar (i.e., confounders), explicitly pushing hateful and benign memes apart.
papers.summary_method:
Model Architecture: Employs a frozen CLIP encoder combined with a trainable MLP to produce joint vision-language embeddings.

Dynamic Example Retrieval: For each training meme (the "anchor"), the system retrieves the pseudo-gold positive and hard negative examples from the current embedding space, which is updated every epoch.

Loss Function: Uses a combined loss:

Standard Cross-Entropy Loss: For the final logistic classification.

Novel RGCL Loss: An auxiliary loss to refine the embedding space. It pulls the anchor closer to the pseudo-gold positive and pushes it away from hard negatives and random in-batch negatives.

Retrieval-Based KNN Classifier: After training, a KNN classifier can be used. It allows the system to be updated simply by adding new examples to the retrieval database without full retraining, which is ideal for evolving online content.
papers.summary_conclusion:
State-of-the-Art Performance: Outperforms previous models on the HatefulMemes dataset with an AUROC of 87.0, surpassing even much larger models like Flamingo-80B.

Superior Cross-Domain Generalization: A model trained on one dataset (HarMeme) generalizes to an unseen dataset (HatefulMemes) using the KNN classifier, a capability not seen in other models.

Practical, Easily Updatable System: The KNN classifier enables easy updates by adding new examples, a feature absent in systems requiring full retraining.

Generalizable Across Domains: The method's effectiveness extends to other tasks like offensive content and sarcasm detection, showing its broad utility.
papers.summary_limitation: The paper acknowledges that the definition of "hate speech" can be subjective and is an ongoing societal debate. It also suggests a specific area for future improvement: the current model can struggle with subtle facial expressions, which could be addressed by a more powerful vision encoder.
papers.conference: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
papers.analogy_summary: 该文章就像给AI配备了一位“案例对比教官”——通过反复比对相似与相反的模因实例，教会它精准揪出那些伪装巧妙、仅存细微差别的仇恨模因。
papers.abstract: Hateful memes have emerged as a significant concern on the Internet. Detecting hateful memes requires the system to jointly understand the visual and textual modalities. Our investigation reveals that the embedding space of existing CLIP-based systems lacks sensitivity to subtle differences in memes that are vital for correct hatefulness classification. We propose constructing a hatefulness-aware embedding space through retrieval-guided contrastive training. Our approach achieves state-of-theart performance on the HatefulMemes dataset with an AUROC of 87.0, outperforming much larger fine-tuned large multimodal models. We demonstrate a retrieval-based hateful memes detection system, which is capable of identifying hatefulness based on data unseen in training. This allows developers to update the hateful memes detection system by simply adding new examples without retraining — a desirable feature for real services in the constantly evolving landscape of hateful memes on the Internet.
papers.notes: Comment: ACL 2024 Main. The code is available from: https://github.com/JingbiaoMei/RGCL

papers.title: AdamMeme: Adaptively Probe the Reasoning Capacity of Multimodal Large Language Models on Harmfulness
papers.authors: Zixin Chen, Hongzhan Lin, Kaixin Li, Ziyang Luo, Zhen Ye, Guang Chen, Zhiyong Huang, Jing Ma
papers.summary_motivation: To overcome the limitations of static, accuracy-based benchmarks that cannot keep up with the fast-evolving nature of harmful memes, by proposing a flexible framework for dynamically evaluating mLLMs.
papers.summary_innovation: The first model-centric evaluation framework for harmful meme understanding that uses multi-agent collaboration to adaptively generate challenging test cases, moving beyond simple accuracy metrics. It focuses on a model's reasoning ability to discern nuanced harmfulness.
papers.summary_method: A three-stage agent-based pipeline: (1) Harmfulness Mining: Agents form a taxonomy of harm categories (e.g., race, gender); (2) Model Scoring: A "scorer agent" evaluates the target model's analysis against an AI-generated reference; (3) Iterative Refinement: A "refiner agent" modifies meme text to create harder samples that expose the model's specific weaknesses.
papers.summary_conclusion: AdamMeme effectively reveals varying performances and fine-grained vulnerabilities across different mLLMs (e.g., LLaVA, Qwen, GPT-4o). It provides in-depth, analytical insights into their reasoning capacities on harmfulness, marking a shift from model-agnostic to model-centric evaluation.
papers.summary_limitation: Limitations include the inherent bias of using GPT-4o as the primary agent controller, potential lack of representativeness from current datasets, and the difficulty in evaluating models with strong safety mechanisms (e.g., Claude). Future Work will focus on improving evaluation reliability, expanding to more diverse and dynamic data sources, and developing methods to assess more safety-aligned models.
papers.analogy_summary: 该文章就像一位“刁钻考官”，通过多智能体合作不断给AI模型升级模因考题，精准测出它在识别有害内容上的真实推理短板。
papers.abstract: The proliferation of multimodal memes in the social media era demands that multimodal Large Language Models (mLLMs) effectively understand meme harmfulness. Existing benchmarks for assessing mLLMs on harmful meme understanding rely on accuracybased, model-agnostic evaluations using static datasets. These benchmarks are limited in their ability to provide up-to-date and thorough assessments, as online memes evolve dynamically. To address this, we propose AdamMeme, a flexible, agent-based evaluation framework that adaptively probes the reasoning capabilities of mLLMs in deciphering meme harmfulness. Through multi-agent collaboration, AdamMeme provides comprehensive evaluations by iteratively updating the meme data with challenging samples, thereby exposing specific limitations in how mLLMs interpret harmfulness. Extensive experiments show that our framework systematically reveals the varying performance of different target mLLMs, offering in-depth, fine-grained analyses of modelspecific weaknesses. Our code is available at https://github.com/Lbotirx/AdamMeme.

papers.title: Follow-Your-Emoji-Faster: Towards Efficient, Fine-Controllable, and Expressive Freestyle Portrait Animation
papers.authors: Yue Ma, Zexuan Yan, Hongyu Liu, Hongfa Wang, Heng Pan, Yingqing He, Junkun Yuan, Ailing Zeng, Chengfei Cai, Heung-Yeung Shum, Zhifeng Li, ...
papers.summary_motivation: The main goal of this work is to create an efficient and expressive system for animating static portraits into dynamic videos. The key challenges addressed are preserving the unique identity of the reference portrait, accurately transferring a full range of target expressions, and ensuring long-term temporal consistency in the generated animation. The paper aims to overcome the limitations of existing GAN and diffusion-based methods, which often produce artifacts, struggle with identity preservation, and are too slow for practical use.
papers.summary_innovation:
Expression-Aware Landmarks: A new motion representation derived from 3D facial keypoints. This prevents identity leakage and supports exaggerated expressions, even for non-human subjects like cartoons or animals.

Facial Fine-Grained Loss: A novel loss function that uses facial and expression masks to force the model to focus on subtle expression details and fine-grained appearance, leading to higher fidelity.

Taylor-Interpolated Cache (TIC): A training-free caching strategy that accelerates inference by 2.6x without sacrificing quality, a significant breakthrough for real-time applications.

Progressive Generation Strategy: A technique to generate stable long-duration animations, tackling the temporal instability common in diffusion-based video models.

EmojiBench++: A new, comprehensive benchmark with diverse portraits and driving videos to better evaluate freestyle animation performance.
papers.summary_method:
Motion Representation: 3D facial landmarks (using MediaPipe) are projected into 2D to create "expression-aware" motion signals. These are used to guide the animation.

Identity Preservation: The system uses an appearance network to extract features from the reference portrait. These features, along with CLIP image tokens, are injected into the Stable Diffusion UNet via cross-attention to preserve the subject's identity.

Training Objective: The model is trained with the standard diffusion loss plus the proposed "facial fine-grained loss," which enforces high-quality generation of masked facial regions (e.g., eyes, mouth).

Efficient Inference: During inference, a "progressive generation strategy" first synthesizes keyframes and then interpolates intermediate frames. The TIC algorithm dynamically caches and updates UNet features using a Taylor expansion, reducing redundant computations and achieving a 2.6x speedup.
papers.summary_conclusion:
Superior Performance: The proposed method significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art approaches in both animation quality and controllability across diverse portrait types.

High Efficiency: The TIC acceleration enables a 2.6x lossless speedup, making the system practical for real-world applications.

Open Source & Benchmark: The authors are releasing the code, training dataset, and EmojiBench++ to foster further research in the community.
papers.summary_limitation: The authors acknowledge a primary limitation: the model struggles to generate fine details (like a cartoon bear's tongue and teeth) for specific domains, which they attribute to dataset bias. Future work may focus on addressing this by curating a more diverse dataset and exploring methods to improve generalization to unseen portrait styles and anatomical details.
papers.analogy_summary: 该文章就像一位“表情迁移魔法师”，能精准捕捉任何夸张表情，快速“附身”到卡通、真人甚至动物肖像上，同时保持原汁原味的身份特征和丝滑流畅的长视频动画。
papers.abstract: We present Follow-Your-Emoji-Faster, an efficient diffusion-based framework for freestyle portrait animation driven by facial landmarks. The main challenges in this task are preserving the identity of the reference portrait, accurately transferring target expressions, and maintaining long-term temporal consistency while ensuring generation efficiency. To address identity preservation and accurate expression retargeting, we enhance Stable Diffusion with two key components: a expression-aware landmarks as explicit motion signals, which improve motion alignment, support exaggerated expressions, and reduce identity leakage; and a fine-grained facial loss that leverages both expression and facial masks to better capture subtle expressions and faithfully preserve the reference appearance. With these components, our model supports controllable and expressive animation across diverse portrait types, including real faces, cartoons, sculptures, and animals. However, diffusion-based frameworks typically struggle to efficiently generate long-term stable animation results, which remains a core challenge in this task. To address this, we propose a progressive generation strategy for stable long-term animation, and introduce a Taylor-interpolated cache, achieving a 2.6X lossless acceleration. These two strategies ensure that our method produces high-quality results efficiently, making it user-friendly and accessible. Finally, we introduce EmojiBench++, a more comprehensive benchmark comprising diverse portraits, driving videos, and landmark sequences. Extensive evaluations on EmojiBench++ demonstrate that Follow-Your-Emoji-Faster achieves superior performance in both animation quality and controllability. The code, training dataset and benchmark will be found in https://follow-your-emoji.github.io/.
papers.notes: Comment: accepted by IJCV2025. project page:https://follow-your-emoji.github.io
